questions.q: The intrinsic pathway of coagulation is initiated by:
options: A. Tissue thromboplastin (Factor III) released from damaged tissue cells
B. Contact between Factor XII (Hageman factor) and exposed subendothelial collagen
C. Thrombin formation via the extrinsic pathway only
D. Platelet plug formation blocking the vessel
E. Vitamin K-dependent factor activation

questions.q: The extrinsic pathway of coagulation is activated by:
options: A. Contact of blood with exposed subendothelial collagen (Factor XII)
B. Tissue factor (Factor III/thromboplastin) released from damaged extravascular cells
C. Factor XII activation in the blood
D. Platelet ADP and TXA2 release
E. Fibrinogen conversion to fibrin

questions.q: The final common pathway of coagulation involves:
options: A. Platelet aggregation directly creating the fibrin clot
B. Factor X activation → prothrombinase complex → prothrombin → thrombin → fibrinogen → fibrin clot
C. Factor XII activation only
D. ADP release from platelets
E. Vitamin K binding to calcium ions only

questions.q: Fibrinolysis, which dissolves a blood clot after vessel repair, is accomplished by:
options: A. Activation of the coagulation cascade in reverse
B. Platelet plug retraction and contraction
C. Plasmin (activated from plasminogen by tissue plasminogen activator/tPA) digesting fibrin
D. Conversion of fibrin back to fibrinogen
E. Vasoconstriction reducing blood flow to the clot

questions.q: Vitamin K is essential for blood clotting because it:
options: A. Activates platelet adhesion to collagen
B. Is required for the liver synthesis of several clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X) via carboxylation
C. Converts fibrinogen to fibrin directly
D. Activates plasminogen to plasmin
E. Stimulates thromboxane A2 production from platelets

questions.q: Thrombocytopenia refers to:
options: A. Excess platelets in circulation
B. Abnormally low platelet count, increasing risk of bleeding and petechiae
C. Platelet activation without forming a stable plug
D. A clotting factor deficiency
E. Excessive fibrin deposition in vessels

questions.q: Hemophilia A is caused by a deficiency of:
options: A. Vitamin K
B. Platelets (thrombocytopenia)
C. Factor VIII
D. Fibrinogen (Factor I)
E. Von Willebrand factor

questions.q: Iron-deficiency anemia is characterized by:
options: A. Large, macrocytic RBCs with immature nuclei
B. Destruction of RBCs by antibodies (hemolytic)
C. Small, hypochromic (pale, microcytic) RBCs with reduced hemoglobin content
D. Bone marrow failure to produce any blood cells
E. Abnormal hemoglobin that polymerizes under low-O2 conditions

questions.q: Megaloblastic (pernicious) anemia results from deficiency of vitamin B12 or folic acid, producing:
options: A. Small, hypochromic RBCs
B. Large, immature (megaloblastic) RBCs that fail to mature properly due to impaired DNA synthesis
C. Increased RBC destruction by the spleen
D. Deficient iron incorporation into heme
E. Sickle-cell hemoglobin polymerization

questions.q: Sickle cell disease is caused by:
options: A. Iron deficiency reducing hemoglobin production
B. A point mutation in the beta-globin gene producing HbS, which polymerizes under low-O2 conditions, distorting RBCs
C. Vitamin B12 deficiency impairing DNA synthesis
D. Autoimmune destruction of erythrocytes
E. Excess erythropoietin causing RBC overproduction

questions.q: A Type A recipient receives Type B blood in a transfusion. The result is:
options: A. No reaction because both are ABO blood types
B. The recipient's pre-formed anti-B antibodies attack the donor's Type B RBCs, causing hemolysis
C. The recipient produces new anti-A antibodies as a delayed reaction
D. The donor's anti-B antibodies attack the recipient's cells
E. Rh factor incompatibility triggers the primary reaction

questions.q: Type AB individuals are considered universal recipients for packed RBCs because:
options: A. Their blood has both A and B antigens on RBCs but neither anti-A nor anti-B antibodies in plasma
B. Their blood has no surface antigens on RBCs
C. Their antibodies can neutralize any foreign RBC
D. They produce high titers of IgG antibodies
E. Their RBCs have a unique protective surface coating

questions.q: Which leukocyte is the FIRST to arrive at a site of acute bacterial infection?
options: A. Eosinophils
B. Basophils
C. Lymphocytes
D. Neutrophils
E. Monocytes

questions.q: The three steps of hemostasis in correct order are:
options: A. Coagulation → vascular spasm → platelet plug
B. Platelet plug → coagulation → vascular spasm
C. Vascular spasm → platelet plug (primary hemostasis) → coagulation cascade (secondary hemostasis)
D. Coagulation → platelet plug → vascular spasm
E. Vascular spasm → coagulation → platelet plug

questions.q: Heparin, an anticoagulant, works by:
options: A. Dissolving existing clots via activation of plasmin
B. Activating antithrombin III, which inhibits thrombin and other clotting factors
C. Directly preventing platelet aggregation
D. Promoting vasoconstriction to reduce bleeding
E. Increasing fibrinogen production by the liver

questions.q: The plasma protein that is converted to insoluble fibrin threads forming the clot scaffold is:
options: A. Albumin
B. Immunoglobulin (antibody)
C. Fibrinogen (Factor I)
D. Plasminogen
E. Thrombin

questions.q: Type O-negative blood is often called the universal donor for RBCs because:
options: A. Type O-positive cells have extra antigens that protect them from attack
B. O-negative RBCs lack both ABO antigens and the Rh (D) antigen, minimizing transfusion reactions in any recipient
C. O-negative plasma contains no antibodies whatsoever
D. The anti-A and anti-B antibodies in donated blood are harmless at transfusion volumes
E. O-negative blood contains only plasma without formed elements

questions.q: The process by which leukocytes squeeze between endothelial cells of capillary walls to enter infected tissues is called:
options: A. Chemotaxis
B. Phagocytosis
C. Diapedesis (emigration)
D. Opsonization
E. Margination

questions.q: Eosinophils are most important in defense against:
options: A. Acute bacterial infections
B. Viral infections
C. Parasitic infections (helminths) and allergic responses
D. Intracellular fungal infections
E. Tumor cell destruction

questions.q: In aplastic anemia, the bone marrow fails to produce adequate numbers of:
options: A. Red blood cells only
B. White blood cells only
C. Platelets only
D. All formed elements (RBCs, WBCs, and platelets)
E. Plasma proteins

questions.q: Clotting factor II (prothrombin) is converted to thrombin by:
options: A. Direct activation by tissue factor (TF)
B. The prothrombinase complex (Factor Xa + Va + Ca2+ + phospholipid)
C. Factor XII activation
D. Fibrin cross-linking by Factor XIII
E. Vitamin K directly

questions.q: An immature erythrocyte that still contains ribosomes (visible as a reticular pattern) and circulates in blood for 1-2 days before fully maturing is called a:
options: A. Erythroblast
B. Reticulocyte
C. Megaloblast
D. Normoblast
E. Proerythroblast

questions.q: The normal life span of an erythrocyte is approximately:
options: A. 7–10 days
B. 30–60 days
C. 90–120 days
D. 1 year
E. Several years (they do not turn over significantly)

questions.q: Bilirubin is produced from:
options: A. Leukocyte breakdown products
B. The heme portion of hemoglobin when old erythrocytes are destroyed (primarily in the spleen and liver)
C. Platelet granule contents during activation
D. Fibrin clot dissolution products
E. Plasma protein catabolism in the liver

questions.q: The normal hematocrit (packed cell volume) in a healthy adult male is approximately:
Monocytes leaving the bloodstream differentiate into ________ in peripheral tissues.
options: A. Neutrophils
B. Basophils
C. Macrophages
D. Mast cells only (tissue equivalent of basophils)
E. Dendritic cells and natural killer cells exclusively

questions.q: The normal WBC count in a healthy adult is approximately:
options: E. 50/μL

questions.q: Von Willebrand factor (vWF) plays a critical role in hemostasis by:
options: A. Activating the extrinsic coagulation pathway
B. Converting fibrinogen to fibrin
C. Mediating platelet adhesion to exposed subendothelial collagen and stabilizing Factor VIII
D. Inhibiting thrombin activity
E. Activating Factor X in the common pathway

questions.q: Serum differs from plasma in that serum:
options: A. Contains more albumin than plasma
B. Is plasma from which clotting factors and fibrinogen have been removed after clotting
C. Contains red blood cells but no white blood cells
D. Is plasma with all proteins removed
E. Is identical to plasma in composition
